tagline: Calculate deck boards, screws, and joists for your deck

jsonLdWeb.name: Deck Board Calculator
jsonLdWeb.description: Calculate deck boards, linear feet, screws, and joists for any deck project.

mainEntity.name: How many deck boards do I need?

acceptedAnswer.text: Divide the deck width by the board width plus gap, then multiply by the number of boards needed to span the deck length. For a 12×16 ft deck with 5.5" boards and 1/8" gap: 192" ÷ 5.625" = 34.1 → 35 rows. If using 16 ft boards, you need 35 boards. If using shorter boards, calculate the number needed per row.

mainEntity.name: What gap should I leave between deck boards?

acceptedAnswer.text: For wood decking, leave 1/8" to 1/4" between boards for drainage and expansion. Pressure-treated lumber installed wet may need less initial gap as it shrinks while drying. Composite decking typically requires 1/8" side gap and 1/8" to 3/16" end gap per manufacturer specifications.

mainEntity.name: How many screws do I need for decking?

acceptedAnswer.text: Plan on 2 screws per board per joist. For a deck with joists at 16" OC, a 16 ft long board crosses about 13 joists, needing 26 screws. A rough estimate is 350 screws per 100 square feet of decking at 16" joist spacing. A 5 lb box of 2.5" deck screws contains roughly 350 screws.

mainEntity.name: What is the standard deck board width?

acceptedAnswer.text: Standard deck board widths: 5/4×6 lumber has a 5.5" actual face width, 2×6 is also 5.5", and 2×4 is 3.5". Composite boards are typically 5.5" wide. Wider boards cover area faster but are more prone to cupping. Most residential decks use 5.5" boards.

mainEntity.name: How far apart should deck joists be?

acceptedAnswer.text: Standard joist spacing is 16" on-center for most wood and composite decking. Some composite manufacturers allow 12" OC for diagonal installations. 24" OC is only suitable for heavy timber decking (2" thick). Always check the decking manufacturer's requirements for maximum joist spacing.

mainEntity.name: How much waste should I plan for?

acceptedAnswer.text: Plan for 10-15% waste for a rectangular deck with boards running parallel to the longest side. Diagonal patterns need 15-20% extra. Complex shapes with lots of cuts may need 20%+. If using random-length boards, plan for 5-10% waste from end-matching.

howItWorks:
How to Calculate Deck Boards

Deck board calculations determine how many boards cover the deck surface, accounting for the gap between each board.

Rows = Deck Width ÷ (Board Width + Gap)
  • Board rows = deck width in inches ÷ (board face width + gap)
  • Boards per row = ceiling of (deck length ÷ board length)
  • Total boards = rows × boards per row × (1 + waste %)
  • Linear feet = total boards × board length
  • Screws = 2 per board per joist crossing
  • Joists = (deck length in inches ÷ joist spacing) + 1
faqHTML:
Frequently Asked Questions

How many deck boards do I need?

Divide the deck width (in inches) by the board width plus gap, then multiply by the number of boards needed to span the length. For a 12×16 ft deck with 5.5" boards and 1/8" gap: 144" ÷ 5.625" = 25.6 → 26 rows of 16 ft boards.

What gap between deck boards?

Use 1/8" to 1/4" between boards for drainage and expansion. Pressure-treated lumber installed wet needs less initial gap. Composite decking typically requires 1/8" side gap per manufacturer specifications.

How many screws for decking?

Use 2 screws per board per joist. Roughly 350 screws per 100 sq ft at 16" joist spacing. A 5 lb box of 2.5" deck screws has about 350 screws.

What is standard deck board width?

Most residential decks use 5.5" wide boards (5/4×6 lumber or composite). Also available: 3.5" (2×4) for narrow patterns and 7.25" (2×8) for wide plank looks.

How far apart should deck joists be?

Standard is 16" OC for most wood and composite. Some composites require 12" OC for diagonal patterns. Only heavy 2" timber decking is suitable for 24" OC joist spacing.

How much waste should I plan for?

10-15% for rectangular decks with parallel boards. 15-20% for diagonal patterns. 20%+ for complex shapes with many cuts.

sections.title: What Is the Deck Board Calculator?
sections.body: The Deck Board Calculator is a free online tool designed for contractors and DIY builders who need quick, accurate calculations in the construction and building space. By entering your deck length — boards run this direction, deck width, board face width, you get instant results including boards needed, total linear feet, deck area. No formulas to memorize, no spreadsheets to build — just enter your numbers and get the answer in seconds. Whether you're a beginner or experienced professional, this calculator saves you time and eliminates guesswork.

sections.title: Why This Calculation Matters
sections.body: Getting boards needed right can make the difference between success and costly mistakes. In construction and building, small errors compound quickly. Manual calculations are error-prone and time-consuming, especially under pressure. This calculator applies proven formulas used by contractors and DIY builders worldwide, giving you confidence that your numbers are correct. Use it to plan your projects with precision and avoid common pitfalls that trip up beginners.

sections.title: When Should You Use This Calculator?
sections.body: This tool is most useful when you know your deck length — boards run this direction and need to find the right boards needed. It's also great for quick estimates before committing to a decision, and to double-check manual calculations or professional quotes, and when comparing different scenarios side by side. Bookmark this page and come back whenever you need a fast, reliable answer — the calculator is always free and requires no signup.

howToSteps.title: Enter Your Deck Length (ft) — boards run this direction
howToSteps.description: Start by entering your deck length — boards run this direction — this is the primary input for the calculation.

howToSteps.title: Fill In Additional Details
howToSteps.description: Complete the remaining fields: deck width, board face width, gap between boards, board length available, joist spacing, waste allowance. Each value refines the calculation for greater accuracy.

howToSteps.title: Click Calculate
howToSteps.description: Hit the Calculate button to run the numbers. Results appear instantly below.

howToSteps.title: Review Your Results
howToSteps.description: Check your boards needed, total linear feet, deck area. Use these figures to inform your next decision or compare against alternative scenarios.

tips: Double-check your deck length — boards run this direction before calculating — even small input errors can significantly change your results.
Run the calculator with different values to compare scenarios and find the optimal approach for your situation.
Pay attention to both boards needed and total linear feet — they work together to give you the full picture.
Bookmark this page for quick access next time you need to plan your projects.
If you're unsure about your waste allowance, start with a conservative estimate and adjust from there.

faq.question: Is the Deck Board Calculator free to use?
faq.answer: Yes, completely free with no signup required. Use it as many times as you need — there are no limits or hidden fees.

faq.question: How accurate is this calculator?
faq.answer: This calculator uses standard construction and building formulas trusted by contractors and DIY builders. Results are reliable estimates for planning purposes. For critical decisions, we recommend consulting a qualified professional to verify.

faq.question: What deck length — boards run this direction should I enter?
faq.answer: Enter the most accurate deck length — boards run this direction value you have available. If you're estimating, use a conservative figure. You can always run the calculator again with different values to see how changes affect the results.

faq.question: Can I use this calculator on my phone?
faq.answer: Yes, the Deck Board Calculator is fully responsive and works on any device — phone, tablet, or desktop. No app download needed.

faq.question: What does boards needed mean in my results?
faq.answer: The boards needed value represents the primary output of the calculation based on your inputs. Use it as a baseline figure for your construction and building planning and decision-making.
